speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so Lilly, you were saying that Wallenberg came to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to the ghetto, and I translated between Wallenberg, [INAUDIBLE] [? Nielsen, ?] and the Jewish officials. Of course, I can't remember what was said. I was most probably either too young, or not interested, or not involved in the actual--

speaker: interviewer
text: Conversation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Contract-- the contract, what they'd done, whatever--

speaker: interviewer
text: Negotiations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Negotiation. I don't know what-- that I can't remember. I only know that I stood there, and I helped them, and translated. And I only know that I said to them that I would like to leave with them. And I want to bring my brother. And they said, you haven't got time to go home to get your brother, but we can take you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, what happened was, that these 23 people, 12 on this side and 12-- and they took me into the middle because I didn't have any official papers. These people, the others, did have papers, what one they had already printed out front, and they paid for it. I neither paid, nor had I had time or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just grabbed me, and when we were marching through in the dark, because it was the 24th of December and I think it was about 6 o'clock in the evening, we-- the two dozen people sort of on one side the other, and I was crouching in between. And in the crouching position, we went out the gate. So the guards did not see me being down-- down there. We got out, and there was a policeman who was taking us to a protected house, a Swedish protected house. He didn't know whether I was official or not, because once we were out of the gate, he didn't know I got up, I went straight, and he didn't know whether I belonged to the 23, 24, or not. So there was no problem. And we walked quite a bit to get to a certain protected house. Now, because I didn't have any official papers-- official, meaning Wallenberg papers, I had to stay in the building, instead of going down to the cellar. But, anybody who was paying for it, they had to go down the cellar because we got the bombs constantly. I was up on the top floor, third floor, in that particular building, on the 6th of January. So, we arrived there on December. Food I got from them, but I had to take it upstairs or something. I can't recall that food business there. I only know that I had-- I was up there, not in the cellar. And on the 6th of January, we had a sort of a carpet bombing, where I don't know whether the Americans, the English, or the Russians were bombarding Budapest in a heavy way. And I was on the third floor, and I huddled into a corner because I was afraid, when are we going to cop the bomb. And sure enough, we did. I was on my own on that third floor, and the bomb went through the staircase. And when I opened up my eyes, the dust. And when the dust cleared itself, it was the air, the sky. Somehow or other, I got down, and the whole house was evacuated, because that was-- they couldn't have-- have us there anymore. And they took us to another protected-- Swedish protected house quite far away, where we stayed and-- in a cellar. There we were in the cellar. I don't know, I think my papers by then, was not important. But that was 6th of January. And the Russians were already in Budapest, on the borders of the city, so nobody worried about paper-- official papers. There weren't that many Germans around. There were Nazis, Hungarian, the Nyilas. And what we found out then, that in these last two weeks, a lot of these Swedish protected houses were gathered together, in-- the tenants or we thought there-- in-- living in ones, gathered together, took to the Danube, and were shot into the Danube by these Hungarian Nyilas. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, when I heard that, and I was still in one of the protected houses, we were scared that it happens to us, too. But, we heard that not only were some of them shot in, but some of them were rescued by Jewish who were in uniform of the Nyilas. Now, what they've done, they took all these Jews into the ghetto. Because if the ghetto was protected, they were not allowed-- the Nyilas were not allowed to come into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we didn't know. I was standing there and waiting. When am I going to see my grandma, and my auntie, and my-- my brother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what had happened in the meantime, they were one of the houses where the Jewish Nyilas took them into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they survived it. If they would have been with the Nyilas, they would have been in the Danube, dead, like all the others. But they were-- one of them were the Jewish Nyilas in the uniform-- took these inmates and took them to the ghetto, and they were freed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say Jewish Nyilas, do you mean that there were Jews who dressed up in Nyilas uniform--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's exactly what I meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how they got rescued.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It's getting heavy. When we-- when the Russians came in to that part of the city, where I was in that protected house, I was so happy to see the Russians, that they're coming closer. It was a street fight. It was actually from one building to another, and from one street to the other, and I joined them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were living in the protected house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hundreds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hundreds. But, maybe in where I was, in the cellar there in that protected house, there were about 50, and in the next house was 50. And they were all scattered around because there were quite a lot of protected house in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So, when you say that the Russians were street-fighting, does this mean that, in some ways, this was liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was liberation. So, we got liberated-- if-- I can't remember if it were 14th or 16th. And I marched with them, street-fighting, to get, as soon as possible, to the ghetto. Now, that was one of the incidents where I got caught by three Russian soldiers, and by gunpoint, they took me up in an apartment, which was bombarded, the glasses were out of the house, because there was not one house in Budapest which had the glasses in it. And they [INAUDIBLE] raped me then. And that was something which I am surprised, that now, with my old age, I didn't have any hang-ups, human beings-wise, or sexually, or anything. I only know that I begged them to leave me alone because, I said, doctor, doctor. And most probably in Russia, in Russian language, it has not got the same emphasis, not the same meaning as what it has in the English or in the Hungarian language. And they didn't take any notice of me whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean you said doctor, doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, at that time, I don't know whether you're aware of it, the people who were fed by a-- the Jews in the ghetto, or in that protected house, we got something in our food which stops us from menstruating. Now, automatically, when I was out of that ghetto already, for two or three weeks, and lived in that protected house, that was missing out of our-- and I started to get my period, which is not normal, one, but was a brownish. Now, don't forget, I was full with life-- lice. I had the same clothing on. And, I mean the same. From September on boots. So it was September, October, November, December, January. I was full, as I said, with this lice. And my hair, and my body-- I had different lice in my hair and I had different lice in my body on the clothing. I didn't know what water was like for shower or for washing yourself. And it was very unpleasant to know that three guys are there, raping you, when you have a period which is brown and, and ugly and--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, [SIGHS] after these-- well they weren't very satisfied. I didn't cooperate with them, and they left. They said you can take what you want, just showed me that I can take what I want from the flat. And they disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time I got myself together, I got a suitcase. And there were no women. That was the men's apartment. And no, no woman's clothing, but only men. And I've got shoes, and men's suits, thinking I'm going to see Freddy whenever, whether it's one day, or one week, or one month, and then he's got something to put on his back. Well, unfortunately, I never saw him again, but that's beside the point. I took that suitcase, and with that suitcase, I followed the Russians. And I went first to the house where the family was, in the Jewish star house. Nobody was there yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I was walking towards the ghetto, I met Grandma, my auntie, and her little daughter. And, of course, we fell into each other's arms. And I said, where's Freddy? And she said, we haven't seen him since two days. One and a half days ago he was with us, and then he went back to that place where he's sleeping.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, all right. I'm going to go there now, straightaway , and get him and come over. Of course, you can't visualize what it is like, a war-torn city, where, on the electrical wires where the trams were going, there was a body there, and the head here, and the leg there. And it was snowing, and in the snow was red. And horses were dead, and it was absolutely a dreadful sight. When you see your family, certain paths, to see each other, you don't see anything of that. You only see it after or before, but you, you-- at that second, it was only the hugging, that we survived it, and we are here and the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: So Grandma and Auntie went into the star house, and I went opposite direction, to the ghetto, to look for my brother. And they said they haven't seen him the last day and a half. I said, oh shit. Where is he now? So, it-- I went back to the star house, thinking that he might have been, lost our way. And that was in the morning, about 11 o'clock. 10, 11 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't find him. I went back again later in the afternoon and looked, already, on bodies, whether I'd find him. I didn't find him anywhere. And what I heard, that those bodies which were found in the last few days, on the street, they threw them into a mikveh. There was a mikveh, that was a Jewish-- in the ghetto, was a mikveh, and they put all those bodies. Now, I looked everywhere for-- for Freddy. I looked on the streets. I looked in the cemetery. In the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] synagogue, they had the garden there, and they dug in through these ghetto areas, certain bodies, and they put the name outside. So I couldn't find him there either.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, even to the very day, I think what happened was, that when he left Grandma and Auntie in the ghetto two days earlier, on the way home to his place where he was sleeping, he got a shrapnel, which killed him. And they found the body and they threw it into the mikveh. I don't know because that was the only place I didn't look for him. I couldn't. So, that was already the, the liberation by the Russians. And then we had some hard times as far as food is concerned. And Grandma and the little girl had got a sickness, which is diphtheria. I don't know, it's a runner of the bowels, and they had-- we had to have either rice or potatoes for them. Now that didn't exist in Budapest. I know that my auntie and I have tried to go and rob a few shops, which was, at that day, [LAUGHS] if you didn't go to a shop to pinch something-- there was nobody at the counter. I mean, you went just to get food. And-- So, by this stage you were united, the four of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The four of us, Grandma, Auntie, the little one, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. I'm just wondering, Lilly, whether, when you think about the liberation, for you, whether it's a mixture of, of a lot of joy and pain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, the joy was that I-- that whatever, whoever, survived. And the pain was very much that my brother didn't. That is a great-- because that I lost my father, and I lost my mother, [SIGHS] but somehow we two were hanging on to each other, and that was-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you think about the Russians, does that also, the whole sexual assault, is that something that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it didn't bring bad memories. I tell you, frankly, I was thinking all my life, these soldiers were fighting for somebody else's [BACKGROUND NOISE] goodwill, life. They came through from Russia, left their families, and they came to a hostile country, Hungary, where, all right, the Jews were there, to liberate them. But they were already on the way for years, these Russian soldiers. They were frustrated. They found grateful Jews who were kissing them and hugging them to liberate them. And, of course, for them, they're not the most educated people-- for them it was the open door to go and rape us, or have sexual intercourse, or whatever. So I looked at it from that point of view, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I haven't got bad feelings towards Russians, really and truly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have almost, like, a very practical kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mentality to the whole-- yes, I have. And I haven't had, as I said, any hang-ups I have bad memories, and I was affected by the whole year or year and a half what we had concentrated, very bad time in Hungary. But, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I don't consider myself a nutcase, if you know what I mean. Because there are, and I do understand, a lot of people who went through the concentration camps, who went through certain [SIGHS] hostilities, the same way as I did, that they became a bit paranoid with anything what has-- in connection with-- with Germans. They don't want to speak the language anymore, I know of people who went through much worse than I, and they don't want to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't feel this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I feel that we should. It is our duty to let the world and the future know what has happened so it never, ever happens again. I can only tell everybody in the future, they should hang on, don't give up, because I could have given up many times. There were even small incidents in that year where they put me to the wall with a gun at me. And I just hoped and hoped, and, and didn't think that everyone is as bad as certain people. And I survived it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, put a little bit of hope into the future. Some of us do survive to hear-- to tell the story. And I think the youth of the future should not give in. Okay, before we get to some of that, I'm just wondering when and how did you eventually come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when this rape thing happened, and we got to the-- with the family-- oh, I didn't mention. I got gonorrhea through that incident. And I didn't have any medication. There was no medicine at all. So, a month or two after, we got a Red Cross parcel from the Australian relations. And in there was a medication for that little girl and the grandma, penicillin. But they survived it without the penicillin. So the penicillin was used to me and cured me for my gonorrhea. So that was OK. But, my auntie and I were traveling in the country. We took gold or sheets to the country, what the country people didn't have, and they gave us food. So, we took food back to Budapest, and we fed those two, Grandma and my little niece. And we gave-- we brought the food back, so they got a bit better and they built their strength up physically. And the rest of the family who lived in that house in [INAUDIBLE]. And that's how we survived eventually. I met, then, after, a young man. He courted me. We got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came to Australia in 1947. I left Hungary in December, '47. Arrived in Sydney in January, '48, and was married for another two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, altogether, my first marriage lasted three years, when I realized that my husband wasn't a worker. He didn't want to build up a future. He's just sort of a good-looking bludger.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we separated, eventually divorced, and then for 10 years after, I didn't-- 12 years after, I didn't have any marriages. And-- had a relationship for six years. And then, eventually, I married the second time, who is the father of my two children. And that marriage lasted for 10 years. And I'm very happy, in spite of having a divorce in my hand again. But I'm happy that I had the two children. In a way, that was what I lived for. I wanted to have children, of course, under different circumstances, but c'est la vie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Lilly, when you arrived to Australia, did you find that people asked you about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as much the experiences. I did feel a bit of antisemitism. And anti-- not even antisemitism as much, but anti-foreignism, reffos. We were refugees, reffos. There was a stigma to us. There was a name given to us who survived the war, and we came and invaded Australia. And we brought in new habits, eating habits, schnitzel, spaghetti bolognese, coffee, a-- interior decoration. We had different tastes for-- of the Australian, Anglo-Saxon. And we were knocked about it. We were knocked about how we spoke, because we couldn't speak Australian English. Some of the Hungarians spoke English English. And, eventually, we picked up, through work and through learning, and this, that, and the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Lilly, can you tell me whether-- what effect do you think the war experiences have perhaps had on you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you what, I got very tough. I got very tough. I became a man. Through rescuing my auntie, with whom I was-- very often had to help her in those five days, and carry her, so to say, from the sleeping place to a cot. And, somehow, the responsibility was always on my shoulder after, either with her or with my brother. And when my brother wasn't then the responsibility-- because my two husbands weren't really money-spinners, and we just, as immigrants, couldn't make a living of one salary. So, we-- I always had to egg him on to work a bit harder, that we get somewhere. And the same with, with the children. It's-- I always had-- I always had the burden on my shoulder that I have to be the leader. And I'll never forget that my-- the father of my children always said, when there's a big thing, he has to decide. When there are small things, I decide. And in our marriage, never anything big happens. So, he took it in a-- in a humorous way. But I always had the burden that I have to look after certain people who weren't strong enough. And even among friends. I have a girlfriend who lost her husband, and she's very, very vulnerable and weak. And, always, I was the one who were trying to tell her, do this and do that. Eventually, she rebelled. But, she got stronger, too, and today she's standing on her two feet. But I always feel that I have to inspire other people to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you think allowed you to survive the war? How do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what it was. Maybe being strong or being determined that I want to live. And, and I didn't have a very nice youth. That at least something should stay-- that I should experience some pleasure out of life. Toughness. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Luck. Bit of mazel goes far.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, tell me, what-- what effect do you think the experiences may have had on the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got raised very independently. I didn't rub in to them-- they know what I went through-- certain stages, they know. Not everything. And I didn't rub it in ever that I have gone, done this, that, and the other. They know about it, but we didn't have-- I had a very liberal upbringing and I gave them a liberal upbringing. We are not Bible-bashers. We-- both boys had bar mitzvahs. We always keep the High Holy Days. So do the boys, even now that they are 10 years out of the house and out of the country. And-- As far as I can see, I always gave the boys as much as I can, a very mature, independent, self-efficient upbringing. It's not always good, because they left because they're so independent. They left me. They left the country, never to return. But if they're happy, that's all that counts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, in terms of your Jewish feeling, how do you think that's affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Funny enough, that lately-- I don't know whether, they say that when you get older, you become more religious, not because I think, where am I going to go when I leave this Earth. I somehow don't believe in that. But somehow, all my life, I was working always. And I always had to work very, very hard for my living and for the children's living.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that occupation, Lilly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I learned dressmaking. And then, eventually, I had my own clothing factory. And I did do some RR work, where I was very happy. And I remember that, when I separated, and my husband-- my husband said that I'm going to break the bank, sort of, meaning that I won't be able to survive it on my own with the children, on my own salary. That I'm going to go bankrupt. Well, I didn't, but that's besides the point. I brought up those two boys financially, right through till they became 18 years of age, where they helped a little bit to it. And I always had the feeling, proud feeling, that these kids, I did a good job. They brought-- they were brought up-- one was born in 1960, the other in '64, for they're now around the age where it was very hard to bring up two boys on your own, with a working mother. And it-- they're not dropouts. They're educated. They are not bludgers. They work. They are not drug addicts. So I feel that I did a good job, as much as I did. Even with religion, they do feel Jewish to the very day, even they're out of the Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: One. Just been born about six, eight months ago. And I'm very happy with them. I'm very proud Yiddish grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And Lilly, just a couple of final things. I'm wondering whether any of your experiences have had a long-term effect, such that maybe you have dreams or flashbacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have flashbacks. I have dreams. But, funny enough, nothing to do with the war. It is dreams, and I don't know, is it my medication which I am under, that I have every night. Something-- it's not unpleasant dreams. It is dreams-- and when I have my eyes closed, I could still see what's happened in my dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, are these dreams that relate to the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you ever have dreams that relate to the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is something which I'm very surprised. Most probably, again, my strength is-- which is the toughness, that it didn't affect me so much like other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now that it's, you know, 50 years since the war, what message do you think you have for your children, grandchildren, and future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like [SIGHS] them to stay Jewish. I don't say that they have to be Bible-bashers, but stay Jewish. Have the feeling, more to the race as to the religion. There's a way of life to be Jewish. There is a way of feeling to be Jewish. And I don't know whether, has it got anything to do with my upbringing from Grandma, because she put this Jewishness into me, not my mother. And I still, to the very day, use words which I have heard from my grandmother 50 years ago. And maybe I even teaching my children to-- these lang-- these words. But I feel that it's important to keep-- keep it within yourself, and to transport it into your children, into my grandchildren, et cetera, et cetera, certain habits what we had and certain ways we are living.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, despite the fact that, because of being Jewish, you went through such [INTERPOSING VOICES] trauma, how does that-- how do you explain that that's something--

speaker: interviewee
text: Makes us stronger to survive it. Makes it stronger, more determined. We are a race which we should continue having. We should be--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you-- so, you feel very strongly about your Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely. I wouldn't-- I wouldn't-- I'm happy to be Jewish. Many times, you make a joke at, whether you're happy or not, you're still Jewish, so might as well be happy and proud. And that's what I am. I wouldn't like to be anything else, as I wouldn't like to be anything else as a female. In spite of having taken over quite a long years, a male role. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] circumstances brought me to it. But I'm very happy to be a Jewish grandmother, and would like to live for a long while yet to see some more of my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Lilly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pleasure.
